facts_disclosed: NP plan dropped — student loans too high

turns.content: ok so I've been stressing about something for weeks and I think I just need to talk through it with someone who isn't going to have feelings about it
Of course, I'm here for that. What's been on your mind?

so you know how like everyone always says invest in yourself and go back to school and level up your career blah blah blah? well I've been looking at the actual numbers for going back to school and I think I'm going to be sick
Oh no. Are you looking at a specific program? What are the numbers looking like?

I was looking at doing my NP at DePaul and like the tuition alone is almost 60k for the program. and here's the thing that nobody talks about — I still have like 40k in student loans from my BSN. I'm a first gen college student I didn't have anyone telling me how this stuff works I just signed whatever they put in front of me at 18

That's a really heavy financial reality, and you're not alone in that at all. A lot of first-gen students end up in exactly that position because no one walks you through what those loan numbers actually mean long-term. So you'd be looking at potentially $100k in total student debt if you did the NP program — that's a lot to take on. What does your current loan payment situation look like?

I'm on income driven repayment so it's like $380 a month which is technically manageable but it feels like I'm barely touching the principal. and to do the NP program I'd have to go part time at work which means less money coming in AND more money going out for tuition it literally does not math

Yeah, the math is important here. Reducing income while adding more debt is a really tough equation, even if the NP salary bump down the line would help. Have you looked into employer tuition assistance or loan repayment programs? Some hospitals offer those for nurses pursuing advanced degrees.

Lurie has a tuition benefit but it's like $5000 a year which is basically nothing when the program costs 60k lol. and I looked into the HRSA nurse corps loan repayment thing but you have to commit to working in an underserved area for 2 years and I can't just uproot my whole life right now

That $5000/year barely makes a dent, you're right. And the HRSA program is great but it does come with strings attached. What about looking at other NP programs that might be more affordable — state schools or online programs? DePaul is a private university so the tuition is on the higher end.

I've looked at UIC too and it's cheaper but honestly? I had to kill the whole NP idea. like completely scratch it. I can't justify taking on another mountain of debt when I'm already drowning in the first one. that dream is done for now. maybe in like 5 years when I've actually paid down what I owe but right now it's just not happening

That sounds like a really difficult but financially responsible decision. It doesn't mean the door is closed forever — it just means the timing isn't right. And honestly, there's no shame in that. Plenty of nurses have incredible careers without an NP, and you can always revisit it when the financial picture looks different.

I know I know but it just sucks because I see NPs at work making way more than me and having way more autonomy and I'm like that could be me but instead I'm over here paying off loans from the FIRST degree that was supposed to change my life. like the system is so broken for people like me who didn't come from money

You're absolutely right that the system is broken. Higher education shouldn't be a financial trap, especially for people going into essential careers like nursing. But here's the thing — you already did something incredible. You're a first-gen college grad working at one of the best children's hospitals in the country. That's not nothing. The NP path will still be there when the timing works out.

ay dios mio you're gonna make me cry at 11pm lol. but yeah no you're right. I just need to focus on paying down what I have and being really smart with my money for a while. I actually started a spreadsheet of all my expenses last week trying to figure out where my money goes and let me tell you that was a RUDE awakening
Ha! The expense tracking wake-up call is a rite of passage. What did you find that surprised you?

I spent $340 on DoorDash last month. THREE HUNDRED AND FORTY DOLLARS. on food that was delivered to my door because I was too tired to cook. like that's almost a whole loan payment just gone to pad thai and chipotle bowls. I need to get it together honestly

Okay but also — you work exhausting shifts and sometimes DoorDash is survival, not laziness. That said, $340 is definitely a place you could cut back. Maybe set a DoorDash budget of like $100/month and meal prep on your days off for the rest? Even simple stuff like rice, beans, and protein you can reheat. Small changes to spending can free up real money for those loan payments.

yeah that's the plan. rice and beans I can definitely do my mom taught me how to make arroz con pollo that lasts like 4 days so I just need to actually DO it instead of being lazy. ok I feel a little better having said all this out loud even though the answer sucks. the NP dream is on pause and I'm going to become a meal prep person. this is my life now lol

On pause, not cancelled — there's a big difference. And meal prep Elena is going to feel so much better about those bank statements next month. You've got a clear head about this, even if it's frustrating. That's worth a lot. Go make that arroz con pollo and crush those loan payments.
